channel.title: Projects on My New Hugo Project
channel.description: Recent content in Projects on My New Hugo Project

item.title: Hydra 83
item.description:
One board. Two protocols. More to come.

What Is It?#

The Hydra 83 is a custom 83-key mechanical keyboard that can switch between USB HID and PS/2 via a hardware toggle. The name comes from two things:

  • 83 — the total key count
  • Hydra — the multi-headed creature of myth, representing the board’s ability to switch between protocols. In PS/2 mode it’s a standard PS/2 keyboard; in USB mode it’s a USB keyboard that also accepts a PS/2 mouse on the PS/2 connector

The Hydra is intended to be a series. Future versions may add an integrated USB hub, additional protocol modes, or other switching features.

item.title: TODO
item.description:
• add VPS for when jamming

Project LOCUST#

Localized Operation and Coordination Unit for Swarm Tactics — LOCUST

Overview#

A military-grade drone swarm system built around two drone types, purpose-built for reconnaissance, surveillance, tunnel/underground operations, and target tracking. Optimized for anti-terrorism and hostile-environment field use.

  • Async drones (×4, belt-worn + backpack auto-launch) — full sensor suite, independently capable, used for recon, tunnel ops, dark environments, through-wall sensing, object deployment, and directed surveillance.
  • Sync drones (×12, suitcase) — vision-first, centrally coordinated, used for perimeter coverage, formation surveillance sweeps, multi-angle subject lock, and distributed CSI sensing mesh.

Both fleets connect to a computer backpack worn by the operator. The backpack features a motorized auto-launch rack that flips up on command and deploys async drones automatically — no manual handling needed. A head-mounted display (HMD) shows drone feeds and controls. Motion gloves (or Quest 3 hand tracking) allow precision manual control. Solar panels on the suitcase and backpack extend field endurance without shore power.

item.title: Drone Swarm Project — Design Notes v3.1
item.description:
Localized Operation and Coordination Unit for Swarm Tactics — LOCUST

TODO#

Okay, so this will be more military-based instead of for commercial use cases.

  1. Backpack installed drone with a good camera night vision and thermal imaging and strong connection so you can scan tunnels and shit (Meaning, instead of having it built warm for charging, it should be probably on the backpack for comfortability. Or maybe we could also have just extra ones on the backpack. We should also make it that the backpack has a motor that will, like, flip up so that the drones can go on, like, take off automatically)
  2. add also a holder for that can release an object with a command.
  3. More military-grade equipment, meaning, upgrade cameras instead of them being those cheap ones to be better quality, that can do dual mode for all cameras, and maybe even, like, for dual mode, um, meaning, seeing in the dark and, like, night vision and regular vision. Maybe I’ll end up, we’ll have to get an extra camera that will be able to do on all the drones, uh, heat signature mapping. And, uh, yeah, once again, military-grade equipment, not, uh, cheap stuff. I can’t have this fail on me.
  4. Add suicide capability. (meaning for async and synchronous drones, meaning that you can just independently assign each drone a target, and well, the rest is history.)

Synchronous Drones (Sync)#

Role: Surrounding the perimeter of the building while maintaining formation.

item.title: Drone Swarm Project — Design Notes v3
item.description:
Overview#

A multi-functional drone swarm system built around two drone types:

  • Async drones — full sensor suite, independent AI capable, worn on a belt, used for personal recon, surveillance, podcasting, forensics, dark environments, pointing/scanning tasks, and WiFi through-wall sensing.
  • Sync drones — stripped-down vision-first design, centrally coordinated, stored in a suitcase, used for formations, light shows, multi-angle filming, perimeter coverage, group recon, and distributed WiFi sensing meshes.

Both fleets connect to a central computer backpack worn by the operator. A head-mounted display (HMD) shows drone feeds and controls. Motion gloves allow precision manual control of any individual drone. Solar panels on the suitcase, backpack, and belt extend field endurance without access to shore power.

item.title: Drone Swarm Project — Design Notes v2
item.description:
Overview#

A multi-functional drone swarm system built around two drone types:

  • Async drones — full sensor suite, independent AI capable, worn on a belt, used for personal recon, podcasting, forensics, dark environments, and pointing/scanning tasks.
  • Sync drones — stripped-down vision-first design, centrally coordinated, stored in a suitcase, used for formations, light shows, multi-angle filming, perimeter coverage, and group recon.

Both fleets connect to a central computer backpack worn by the operator. A head-mounted display (HMD) shows drone feeds and controls. Motion gloves allow precision manual control of any individual drone.

item.title: Sphere Controller — Setup Guide
item.description:
Block Diagram#

![Pasted image 20260323163455](Pasted image 20260323163455.png)

Wiring# 

Pico GPIO 4 (SDA) ──┬── MPU6050 SDA
                    └── SSD1306 SDA

Pico GPIO 5 (SCL) ──┬── MPU6050 SCL
                    └── SSD1306 SCL

Pico 3V3 ───────────┬── MPU6050 VCC
                    └── SSD1306 VCC

Pico GND ───────────┬── MPU6050 GND
                    └── SSD1306 GND
                    └── All button commons

GPIO 14 ── ORBIT button ── GND
GPIO 15 ── PAN   button ── GND
GPIO 16 ── HOME  button ── GND
GPIO 17 ── MODE  button ── GND

Note: leave XDA and XCL unconnected
